metadata.title: Peer Review Decoded: What Reviewers Actually Do Before They Read Your Paper

beats.narration_text: Sawadee — this is Liam, in for Bear. Based on the essay 'How Scientific Peer Review Actually Works' from NortheasternISE's Substack, for NortheasternISE / Humanitarians AI. How does peer review actually work? Reviewers do not start with your abstract. They start with an ethics and conflict-of-interest check. Then the inverted pyramid — most critical issues first, minor issues last. Then the three R's: replicable, repeatable, robust. The methods section is where papers live or die. The rest is legible once you know the system.

props.segment: Peer Review Decoded: What Reviewers Actually Do Before They Read Your Paper
props.command: How does peer review actually work?
output: Step 1: ethics and conflict-of-interest check — before reading the abstract.
Step 2: inverted pyramid — major critiques first, minor last.
Step 3: three R's — replicable, repeatable, robust. Methods section is the battlefield.

beats.narration_text: The inverted pyramid structures the review report, not the reading order. At the top, widest: the executive summary — one to three sentences about the paper's core claim and whether it stands. Below that: major critiques — methodological flaws, unsupported claims, missing controls, interpretive errors that would change the conclusion. Below that: minor critiques — writing clarity, missing references, figure quality, presentation issues. At the tip: editorial suggestions. The reviewer writes in this order. They read in almost the opposite order — methods section first, then results, then discussion, then introduction. The abstract is often last. What the reviewer cares most about is not what the author typically polishes most.

manim.description: Inverted pyramid animates level by level filling from top. Level 1 (widest): 'Executive summary — core claim + verdict'. Level 2: 'Major critiques — methodology, unsupported claims, missing controls'. Level 3: 'Minor critiques — clarity, references, figures'. Level 4 (tip): 'Editorial suggestions'. Each level labeled as it fills.
manim.sparkLine: The widest part is the verdict.

beats.narration_text: The three R's are a test framework, not just vocabulary. Replicable: can the same lab reproduce the result with the same materials and protocol? This tests whether the methods section is complete enough to follow. Repeatable: can a different lab reproduce the result with the same protocol but different materials and personnel? This tests whether the result is an artifact of one setup. Robust: does the result hold under different conditions — different populations, different instruments, different analytical choices? The methods section is where reviewers apply all three tests. If the methods section does not provide enough detail to attempt replication, the paper fails the first test before the results are even read.

animated_deck.description: Three-R test as decision tree. Root: 'peer review test'. Branch 1: Replicable — same lab, same materials, same result? Branch 2: Repeatable — different lab, same protocol, same result? Branch 3: Robust — different conditions, same result? Each branch shows what fails and what passes. Methods section highlighted as where tests are applied.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The three R's are a test framework, not just vocabulary. Replicable: can the same lab reproduce the result with the same materials and protocol? This tests whet

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Reviewers do not start with your abstract — they start with an ethics and conflict-of-interest check. The inverted pyramid structures the review: executive summary, major critiques, minor critiques, editorial suggestions — most critical first. The three R's: replicable, repeatable, robust — applied to the methods section first and with maximum rigor. The methods section is where papers live or die, and reviewers test repeatability there first.

artifactLines: Reviewers start with ethics/conflict check — not the abstract.
Inverted pyramid: executive summary → major critiques → minor critiques.
Three R's: replicable (same lab), repeatable (different lab), robust (different conditions).
Methods section is where papers live or die — reviewers test repeatability there first.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt — read it with me. 'Apply the inverted pyramid framework to a paper you're submitting or reviewing. Write your own review in inverted-pyramid format. What does the executive summary say?' The executive summary is the hardest part — it requires a one-sentence verdict on the core claim. Writing it forces you to decide whether the paper stands. If you cannot write that sentence, you have not yet understood the paper. Use this in your own Claude session on any paper you are working with. Liam, in for Bear.
